behaviors.description: A file placed inside a subdirectory of the watched root is processed, not only files placed directly in the root directory.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is real recursive filesystem watching (directory tree events and watcher registration across nested paths). A fake watcher or direct function call can pass while real OS/watcher behavior misses subdirectory events.
behaviors.test_description: Start the daemon against a real temp directory tree, create a nested subdirectory, write a file into it, and assert exactly one transformed output is produced in the output directory.

behaviors.description: The deduplication logic correctly discards a second processing request for the same file path within a configurable time window.
behaviors.justification: This behavior is local decision logic (path + time-window check). No OS/network/subprocess is required to trigger the real failure mode. Rejection gate: higher levels were considered, but behavior text focuses on dedup logic itself, not filesystem event delivery or watcher handoff.
behaviors.test_description: Call the dedup component twice with the same path and controlled time values; assert first request is accepted and second within the configured window is rejected, then accepted again after window expiry.

behaviors.description: When the operating system emits multiple filesystem events for a single file write, the daemon processes the file exactly once.
behaviors.justification: Behavior explicitly depends on OS event burst semantics. Mocking events can hide duplicate-event patterns and timing seen with real filesystem backends.
behaviors.test_description: Run daemon with a real watched directory, perform one file write known to trigger multiple change/create events, and assert pipeline executes once (single output and/or single processing record).

behaviors.description: If the watched directory is removed and then recreated while the daemon is running, the daemon resumes watching the new directory without a restart.
behaviors.justification: This is an inode/path lifecycle behavior at OS watcher boundary. In-process fakes typically do not reproduce remove/recreate watch invalidation and re-subscription edge cases.
behaviors.test_description: Start daemon on a real directory, delete the watched directory, recreate it at the same path, then add a new file and verify it is processed without restarting the daemon.

behaviors.description: When multiple files arrive at the same time, they are processed in the order determined by their creation timestamps.
behaviors.justification: Ordering depends on real filesystem timestamps and concurrent arrival timing. A mock can mask timestamp resolution/tie behavior and watcher scheduling differences that cause real misordering.
behaviors.test_description: Create multiple files nearly simultaneously in the real watched directory with controlled creation-time differences where possible, then assert observed processing order matches timestamp ordering policy.

behaviors.description: A file that is still being written to when the arrival event fires is not picked up for processing until writing is complete.
behaviors.justification: Failure appears at real filesystem/event timing boundary (partial writes, append-in-progress, close semantics). Stubs usually cannot reproduce true write-in-progress races.
behaviors.test_description: Write a large file in chunks (keeping stream open) in the watched directory, trigger arrival event naturally, and assert daemon delays processing until writer closes and full content is available.
